contents: Clinical Therapeutics/Volume 29, Number 11, 2007 Meta-Analysis of Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis in Medically Ill Patients Abir O. Kanaan, PharmD1,2; Matthew A. Silva, PharmD1,3; Jennifer L. Donovan, PharmD1,4,s; Tara Roy, Np6; and A. Samer AI-Homsi, MD 6 1Department of Pharmacy Practice, Massachusetts College of Pharmacy and Health Sciences, Worceste6 Massachusetts; 2Department of Pharmacy, Saint Vincent Hospital, Worceste6 Massachusetts; 3Department of Family Medicine, University of Massachusetts Medical School, Worceste6 Massachusetts; 4Department of Emergency Medicine, University of Massachusetts Memorial Medical Cente6 Worceste6 Massachusetts; SDepartment of Pharmacy, University of Massachusetts Memorial Medical Cente6 Worceste6 Massachusetts; and 6Department of Hematology/Oncology, University of Massachusetts Memorial Medical Cente6 Worceste6 Massachusetts ABSTRACT Background: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis in medically ill patients has received a level 1A recommendation in previously published clini- cal guidelines. Pharmacologic prophylaxis for VTE in- cludes unfractionated heparin (UFH), low-molecular- weight heparin (LMWH), and fondaparinux. Few direct comparisons between anticoagulants exist in medically ill patients. Objective: This meta-analysis was conducted to as-

sess UFH and LMWH (including the selective factor Xa inhibitor fondaparinux) in the reduction of in-hospital VTE in unselected medically ill patients. Methods: We searched MEDLINE, EMBASE, and the Cochrane Controlled Trials Registry databases from January 1981 through September 2007 (English language) for randomized controlled trials using the following terms: dalteparin, enoxaparin, fondaparin- ux, nadroparin, and heparin. References of included articles and key review papers for additional studies were also searched. Data from studies were included in the analysis if the studies included medically ill pa- tients with risk factors for VTE who had been fol- lowed up for 7 to 21 days. Results: A total of 12,391 patients (of whom 8357

were in placebo-controlled trials) from 9 studies were included. Mean age for the entire cohort was 72.8 years; mean (SD) body mass index, 25.6 kg/m2; and mean (SD) actual body weight, 68.2 kg. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) was significantly reduced with the addition of an LMWH compared with placebo (odds ratio [OR], 0.60; 95% CI, 0.47-0.75; P _< 0.001), but rates of DVT were similar when comparing LMWH with UFH (OR, 0.92; 95% CI, 0.56-1.52). No signifi- cant differences in pulmonary embolism (PE) or death were found among the UFH, LMWH, and placebo groups. LMWH was associated with a significant in- creased risk for minor bleeding compared with placebo (OR, 1.64; 95% CI, 1.18-2.29; P = 0.003). However, no significant difference was found between LMWH and UFH (OR, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.27-1.70). Major bleeding events were similar among all groups: LMWH/fondaparinux versus placebo, OR, 1.65 (95% CI, 0.8-3.4); LMWH/fondaparinux versus UFH, OR, 0.69 (95% CI, 0.29-1.68); LMWH/fondaparinux versus UFH or placebo, OR, 1.16 (95% CI, 0.66- 2.04). Conclusions: This analysis suggests that VTE pro- phylaxis with an LMWH (including fondaparinux) or UFH is effective in reducing the rate of DVT, but this benefit did not extend to enhanced protection against PE. Additionally, LMWH and UFH had similar bleed- ing outcomes. (Clin Ther. 2007;29:2395-2405) Copy- right (c) 2007 Excerpta Medica, Inc.

Key words: venous thromboembolism, prophylaxis, low-molecular-weight heparin, unfractionated hepa- tin, fondaparinux. Printed in the USA. Reproduction in whole or part is not permitted. Copyright (c) 2007 Excerpta Medica, Inc. INTRODUCTION Venous thromboembolism (VTE), including deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE), is a significant medical and financial burden in hospital- ized patients. Historically, hospital-acquired DVT and PE are observed in 10% to 33% of all patients and up to 10% of medically ill patients, 1-8 which translates to increased health care costs with an inflation-adjusted treatment in hospital costs of VTE events ranging from US $12,760 to $18,557 per patient. 9 Moreover, recurrent VTE occurs in 35% of medically ill patients, of whom 23% will develop postthrombotic sydrome, making the long-term complications of VTE a major concern. 1deg,11 Consequently, guidelines from the American College of Chest Physicians provided a level 1A recommendation for primary prophylaxis of VTE in the medically ill population. 12 However, according to the DVT Free Registry, only 42% of inpatients re- ceive appropriate prophylaxis, indicating an under- utilization problem despite Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality recommendations. 13,14

The pathophysiology of VTE is associated mainly with venous stasis and hypercoagulopathy. Major in- dependent risk factors for VTE include paresis, acute infectious disease, congestive heart failure, malignancy, indwelling central venous catheter, varicose veins, and previous thrombosis, is Pharmacologic prophylaxis, aimed at improving venous flow and reducing coagu- lability, has been previously established with low-dose unfractionated heparin (UFH). s,16,17 However, with the advent of low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH), both UFH and LMWH are anecdotally considered therapeutically equivalent for the reduction of VTE in medically ill patients, but data to support similarity be- tween UFH and LMWH have not yet emerged. A newer agent, the selective factor Xa inhibitor fonda- parinux, was reported to have a 46.7% relative risk re- duction of preventing VTE in medically ill patients when compared with placebo. 18 Although current (2004) guidelines do not make recommendations re- garding the role of fondaparinux for the prevention of VTE in medically ill patients, this may evolve with the anticipated updated (2007) guidelines. Moreover, health care professionals recommend prophylaxis regi- mens based on outcomes data generated in placebo- controlled, rather than active-controlled, trials. There are limited controlled trials comparing anticoagulants for thromboprophylaxis in the general population of medically ill patients that provide outcomes data and guidance. Further investigation regarding the outcome benefits of LMWH agents as compared with UFH are thus warranted and should be explored. The objective of this meta-analysis was to systematically review ran- domized controlled trials (RCTs) of VTE prophylaxis in medically ill patients to determine whether there is a difference in efficacy and safety profiles between UFH, LMWH (or fondaparinux), and placebo. MATERIALS AND METHODS Study Identification and Selection

We systematically searched MEDLINE, EMBASE, and the Cochrane Controlled Trials Registry from January 1981 through September 2007 for English- language RCTs evaluating UFH, LMWH, or fonda- parinux for the reduction of in-hospital VTE in the unselected medically ill population using the follow- ing terms: dalteparin, enoxaparin, fondaparinux, nadroparin, and heparin. We searched references of included articles and key review papers for additional studies, and studies were included in the analysis if they included medically ill patients with risk factors for VTE who had been followed up for 7 to 21 days. We clustered dalteparin, enoxaparin, nadroparin, and fondaparinux together in the LMWH/fondaparinux group. Fondaparinux was included with LMWH agents for practicality since fondaparinux has been regarded as an "ultra-LMWH" and because the pharmacoki- netic and pharmacodynamic properties of fondaparin- ux are similar to other LMWH agents, 19 although the US Food and Drug Administration has recently classi- fied it as a selective factor Xa inhibitor. 2deg,21 Efficacy end points of any VTE, DVT, or fatal or nonfatal PE (considered thrombotic events) were selected for comparison, as well as tolerability end points includ- ing major or minor bleeding, fatal bleeding, and VTE- related death. We used the diagnostic criteria for medi- cally ill, VTE, DVT, PE, major bleeding, and minor bleeding provided by each study. Studies failing to re- port drug doses or excluding end points of VTE, fatal or nonfatal PE, or bleeding were excluded from the analysis. Study Quality Three reviewers (A.O.K., M.A.S., and J.L.D.) inde-

pendently evaluated each study for inclusion and ex- clusion criteria and tolerability and efficacy end points and assigned Jadad scores. 22,23 We only included RCTs with a corresponding Jadad score of 3 or greater (maximum score, 5). Discrepancies in Jadad score assignment and inclusion criteria were resolved through consensus and discussion. We abstracted study characteristics including number of patients, pa- tient age, sex, reason for admission, risk factors for VTE events, drug, dose, route, schedule, efficacy end points (VTE, DVT, and PE) and tolerability end points (major and minor bleeding). Data Abstraction and Statistical Analysis As the purpose of this analysis was to compare

the incidence of death, thromboembolic events, and bleeding events in medically ill patients receiving UFH, LMWH/fondaparinux, or placebo, we extract- ed from each study data to create 3 major compari- sons of efficacy evaluating LMWH/fondaparinux against placebo, LMWH/fondaparinux against UFH, or the combination of LMWH/fondaparinux versus UFH or placebo for the reduction of VTE and the oc- currence of new bleeding. Data pertaining to each event were represented as proportions and a meta- analysis was constructed using Comprehensive Meta Analysis version 2.2 (Englewood, New Jersey). We also used the Der Simonian and Laird random-effects model to calculate pooled odds ratios (ORs) and cor- responding 95% CI, with a P value _<0.05 considered statistically significant. 24 The I2 statistic was used to estimate variability from heterogeneity in the effect size of each selected end point. 25 Meta-regression, ac- cording to baseline risk factors, was performed to ex- plore additional relationships between efficacy and tolerability end points. The statistical analysis was performed by one reviewer (M.A.S.), and all authors had access to the statistical database, evaluated the re- suits, and agreed on the data submitted for publication. RES U LTS Literature Search The initial search identified 881 articles, which have been summarized in Figure 1. After examining the ab- stracts and titles, we selected 14 trials for more detailed evaluation, of which 9 fulfilled the predefined inclusion criteria (Table I). ls,26-33 All included reports were RCTs. The 5 excluded trials included patients who failed to achieve a minimum Jadad score of 3, mainly due to lack of randomization. 5,16,17,34,35 Although patients who have experienced stroke are regarded as medically ill, trials that comprised solely stroke patients were excluded as a recently published meta-analysis addresses VTE prophy-

laxis in this population, and the objective of the current study was to examine an unselected medically ill popu- lation as seen in routine clinical practice. 36 Demographic Characteristics The total number of patients included in this analy- sis was 12,391, of which 8357 were in placebo-con- trolled trials. 1s,26-29 The mean age for the entire cohort was 72.8 years, with similar distributions in re- gard to other baseline characteristics (Tables I and II). Mean body mass index was 25.6 kg/m 2 and mean actual body weight was 68.2 kg. Heart failure was re- ported more frequently (29.4%) than acute respirato- ry disease (22.4%) or infection/inflammation (24.7%) (Table II). The majority of trials excluded patients who were mechanically ventilated, had active bleed- ing, renal failure, conditions requiring full anticoagu- lation, or who were pregnant. Thrombotic Events DVT events were significantly reduced with the addi- tion of LMWH/fondaparinux compared with placebo (OR, 0.60; 95% CI, 0.47-0.75; P < 0.001) (Figure 2) and were associated with an absolute risk reduction (ARR) of 1.36% and a number needed to treat (NNT) of 74. Of note, this reduction was driven by dalte- Articles identified (N = 881 ) Excluded studies (n = 867) Not a clinical trial (n = 16) Wrong clinical question (n = 576) Symptomatic VTE (n = 26) Compression boots (n = 12) Surgical patients (n = 200) Stroke patients (n = 10) Trauma patients (n = 12) Spinal cord injury patients (n = 7) Duplicate data (n = 8) Studies evaluated (n = 14) Included studies (n = 9) Excluded studies (n = 5) nous thromboembolism. Reason for Admission No. (%) of Major Risk No. (%) of Patients ~ Factors for VTE Patients ~ Congestive heart failure Acute respiratory disease Acute infection or inflammation Multiple reasons 3066 (24.7) Current or previous cancer 827 (6.7) VTE = venous thromboembolism. *OF 12,391 total patients. parin evaluation as reported by Leizorovicz et al. 28 The remaining 4 LMWH/fondaparinux studies did not find an association with reduced events compared with placebo at 7 to 21 days. 18,26,27,29 No significant difference was found in the incidence of DVT when comparing LMWH/fondaparinux with UFH alone (OR, 0.92; 95% CI, 0.56-1.52), suggesting that LMWH/fondaparinux and UFH are similar in reduc- ing DVT events in medically ill patients (Figure 3). However, when LMWH/fondaparinux was compared with the combination of UFH or placebo, a significant reduction of DVT events was observed (OR, 0.64; 95% CI, 0.51-0.79; P < 0.001), and these data were associat- ed with an ARR of 2.1% and an NNT of 48 (Figure 4). Kanaan et al. LMWH/fondaparinux agents: -44% of patients in the LMWH/fondaparinux group received dalteparin, which may have led to the overestimation of the pro- phylaxis effect of this agent in general. 28 The reduc- tion in the incidence of VTE (DVT and PE) was observed in the LMWH/fondaparinux group com- pared with the placebo group, mainly due to the re- duction of DVT driven by dalteparin. When we compared LMWH/fondaparinux to UFH, there were no significant differences in thrombotic events. Despite a pooling of similar studies, this meta-analysis remained underpowered to detect a significant reduc- tion in PE with LMWH prophylaxis versus placebo through 21 days despite having >8300 patients in the placebo comparison and >12,000 in the combined analysis.

As expected, we observed more minor bleeding events with LMWH/fondaparinux than placebo, but similar rates between LMWH/fondaparinux and UFH. Comparison suggests that treating 150 patients with LMWH/fondaparinux would prevent 2 DVT events and may result in 3 instances of minor bleeding. This comparison suggests that the ratio of benefit/harm favors the routine use of pharmacologic prophylaxis for DVT in the unselected, hospitalized, medically ill population as indicated in current guidelines. Limitations to this meta-analysis should be dis- cussed, including differences across and within stud- ies. First, not all studies used the same criteria for VTE diagnosis or bleeding definitions. In addition, it may not be ideal to group all LMWHs in 1 drug class be- cause there are subtle differences between LMWH agents. We included fondaparinux in the LMWH group because (1) it has similar pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic characteristics to other LMWH agents; (2) it is the only selective factor Xa inhibitor available for comparison; and (3) only 1 trial of fon- daparinux in the medically ill population is available for comparison. Recognizing these limitations, and in the absence of other comparator trials, we felt it was reasonable to combine fondaparinux with other LMWH agents into 1 group. Also, because the focus of this analysis was to look at outcomes, the issue of strict pharmacologic classification was judged to be less important than the analysis of existing evidence of efficacy or safety profiles in the routine clinical prac- tice. Additionally, only English-language studies were included in this meta-analysis. RCTs comparing LMWH agents and fondaparinux are the next step in deter-

mining whether specific agents are clearly associated with a reduction in thrombotic events in the medically ill population. CONCLUSIONS The rates of VTEs appear to be reduced in medically ill patients when addressed with appropriate pharma- cologic prophylaxis. This analysis suggests that VTE prophylaxis with an LMWH (dalteparin, enoxaparin, fondaparinux, or nadroparin) or with UFH is effective in reducing the rate of DVT, but this benefit did not extend to enhanced protection against PE. Additionally, LMWH agents and UFH had similar bleeding out- comes. An additional consideration is that dalteparin, enoxaparin, and nadroparin are associated with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, whereas fonda- parinux is not. Therefore, it is important to consider patient- and institution-specific factors when selecting pharmacologic prophylaxis (ie, renal function, heparin- induced thrombocytopemia risk, and formulary restrictions).
